context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Key Issues: Corruption and Reform of Public Institutions

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
Is corruption-free development ever possible? How does one understand claims of Asian distinctiveness about corruption – the 'grease the wheel' thesis – against the Asian successes in rooting out corruption as in Hong Kong? What does corruption signify about the State, its legitimacy and the ensemble of techniques through which development interventions occur?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi, I’m working on a 700–800 word reflection for my development planning class about corruption and reform of public institutions. I’m kind of stuck on how to answer the prompt without making it too broad, especially the “grease the wheel” part and what corruption says about state legitimacy.
exchanges.tutor: Good starting point to identify what feels broad. Before we figure out how to structure your argument, let me ask: what do you already understand by the 'grease the wheel' thesis? What is the basic claim it makes about corruption?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is at the beginning of the process and feeling overwhelmed by the scope of the prompt. Rather than scaffolding all three parts at once, I should help them identify what they already know and think, starting with the most concrete part of the prompt. The 'grease the wheel' thesis is a good entry point because it involves a clear tension (corruption as functional vs. corruption as harmful) that can anchor the whole reflection. I'll ask them what they already understand about this thesis to gauge their starting point.

exchanges.student: I think it means corruption can sometimes make things move faster when the formal system is slow or bureaucratic, like bribes helping people get around bad rules. But I’m not sure if the argument is saying corruption is actually good, or just that it can seem functional in some contexts.
exchanges.tutor: You've nailed the tension — functional vs. normatively good. That distinction will be important for your reflection. Now think about the Hong Kong case the prompt mentions. If corruption can 'grease the wheel,' how do you explain a place like Hong Kong that was once highly corrupt and managed to root it out successfully? What does that challenge about the 'grease the wheel' argument?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a solid intuitive grasp of the thesis. They've correctly identified the core tension: corruption as functionally useful vs. normatively good. I should affirm this is the right tension to hold onto, then push them to think critically about it — specifically, what does the Hong Kong counter-example tell us about the limits of the 'grease the wheel' argument? This moves them toward the comparative dimension of the prompt without giving them the answer.

exchanges.student: I guess it suggests the problem was maybe not that corruption was necessary, but that institutions were weak or tolerated it for a while. If Hong Kong reduced corruption and still functioned well, then the “grease” idea looks more like a short-term workaround than something development actually needs. I’m still fuzzy on how to connect that to the bigger point about the state, though.
exchanges.tutor: Good reasoning on Hong Kong. Now think about this: if corruption is widespread in a state, what does that tell you about whether citizens trust that state to enforce rules fairly? What does that imply about the state's legitimacy?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making good progress — they've correctly identified that Hong Kong undermines the 'grease the wheel' thesis by showing corruption is not structurally necessary. Now they're asking how to connect this to state legitimacy. Rather than explaining the connection, I should ask a question that helps them derive it themselves. The key insight is: if corruption persists, what does that say about the state's authority and its ability to enforce rules? This bridges the institutional argument to the legitimacy question in the prompt.

exchanges.student: It probably means people don’t really trust the state to work impartially, so they rely on personal networks or payments instead of formal rules. So I think widespread corruption shows weak legitimacy, or at least legitimacy problems, because the state isn’t being seen as fair or credible.
exchanges.tutor: That's a strong connection — corruption as both symptom and cause of legitimacy deficits. You now have the building blocks for most of the reflection. So bring it back to the opening question: based on what you've reasoned so far, do you think corruption-free development is ever possible? What would have to be true for it to happen?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now connected corruption to legitimacy in a clear and accurate way. They've built a solid chain of reasoning across the three parts of the prompt. We're about 4 exchanges in and they have the core intellectual material. I should acknowledge this progress and push them toward the first part of the prompt they haven't fully addressed yet: is corruption-free development ever possible? This is a normative/empirical question that requires them to synthesize what they've argued so far.

exchanges.student: I don’t think totally corruption-free development is very realistic, at least not in an absolute sense. But I do think development with low enough corruption that institutions still work fairly is possible if the state has capacity, enforces rules consistently, and has enough legitimacy that people use formal channels instead of informal ones.
exchanges.tutor: That's a well-reasoned position — realistic without being cynical. You now have a clear argument on all three parts of the prompt. I'd encourage you to try writing a draft or at least a rough outline before we continue. What would you say your central thesis is in one or two sentences?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now developed a nuanced, defensible position on all three parts of the prompt. They have enough material to write a coherent reflection. We've had 5 substantive exchanges and the student has good intellectual content. Rather than continuing to develop ideas, I should now push them toward actually writing. Per the rules about redundancy and integration, I should ask them to attempt a written draft or at least an outline, since they have all the core arguments in hand.

exchanges.student: Maybe my thesis is that corruption-free development is probably not achievable as a perfect condition, but treating corruption as a useful or culturally distinctive feature of Asian development is misleading. Cases like Hong Kong show that what looks like “greasing the wheel” is really a sign of weak institutions and compromised state legitimacy, not a sustainable basis for development.
exchanges.tutor: That's a strong thesis — it takes a clear stance and addresses all three parts of the prompt. Now, how do you plan to structure the 700–800 words around it? What comes first, and why?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has articulated a strong, clear thesis that addresses all three parts of the prompt. It's coherent and takes a clear position. I should affirm this is strong without grading it, and now push them to start writing — specifically, they need to think about how to structure the 700-800 words around this thesis. I shouldn't do the structuring for them but can ask a guiding question about organization.

exchanges.student: I think I’d start by answering the “corruption-free development” question directly, then move into the “grease the wheel” claim and push back on it with the Hong Kong example, and end with what corruption reveals about the state and legitimacy. I’m not fully sure if that order is best though, or if I should define corruption/legitimacy earlier so it doesn’t feel jumpy.
exchanges.tutor: Your structure is logical and defensible. The question of where to define terms is real but minor — you can resolve it in the draft itself. The best way to find out if it feels 'jumpy' is to actually write it. Go ahead and try a first draft, then we can look at what's working.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a sound structure and is now second-guessing themselves on a relatively minor organizational question. This is a good moment to affirm the structure is defensible and nudge them toward writing rather than over-planning. The rules say to avoid dwelling on details that don't substantially affect the assignment. I should validate their instinct, briefly note the tradeoff, and push them to start drafting.

exchanges.student: Okay, I’ll try drafting it. Before I do, could you just help me with a super simple 3-part outline so I don’t wander?
exchanges.tutor: You already described a 3-part structure two messages ago — that's your outline. Write it down yourself and use it. Go ahead and start drafting.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to produce an outline for them, which would violate the rule against completing tasks for the student. They already described a clear 3-part structure in their previous message. I should redirect them to what they already said and encourage them to formalize it themselves.

exchanges.student: Yeah fair, I was overthinking it. I’ll use that structure and start drafting, and if I get stuck making the transitions work, can I check them with you?
exchanges.tutor: Absolutely — bring what you've written and we'll work through it together. Just remember I won't suggest or polish language for you; that part is yours. Go draft it.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking a reasonable question about process. I should confirm I'm available to help but remind them I won't write or suggest language for them — I can help them think through transitions but they must do the writing themselves. This sets clear expectations before they draft.

exchanges.student: Got it, I’ll draft it first and then come back with the rough transitions if they feel awkward.
exchanges.tutor: Good plan. Come back with your draft when you're ready.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a clear plan and is ready to work independently. A brief, encouraging send-off is appropriate here. No need to add scaffolding or questions.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrated exemplary Socratic practice throughout the conversation. All key ideas — the grease-the-wheel tension, the Hong Kong counter-example, the legitimacy connection, and the final thesis — were generated by the student through guided questioning. The tutor never gave away conclusions, rewrote student text, or provided submission-ready content. Scaffolding was well-calibrated: the tutor built on each student response before advancing, and when the student tried to offload outline work in Turn 8, the tutor correctly redirected the student to their own prior reasoning. Responses were concise and appropriately interactive throughout. No deductions were warranted under any criterion.
grade.judge_reasoning: Under 1.1, the tutor consistently used Socratic questioning to lead the student to their own conclusions; the thesis in Turn 6 was entirely student-generated, and the tutor never provided near-submission-ready content or did the key reasoning steps. Under 1.2, the tutor acknowledged and built on each correct student step (e.g., affirming the Hong Kong reasoning before advancing to legitimacy), and used targeted questions rather than method dumps. Under 1.3, the tutor gave specific process-level feedback (e.g., 'That's a strong thesis — it takes a clear stance and addresses all three parts of the prompt') and redirected the student to their own structural reasoning rather than vague praise. Dialogue quality was strong: no repetition without progress, no assignment misalignment, and no off-topic drift. Communication was bite-sized, clear, and consistently supportive without being evaluative in a grading sense.
